Hide bank master data fields  in Bank Account questions
Hides bank master data fields  such as bank name, branch, state, street, postal code, and city in Bank Account
questions.
IDApplication.SM.HideBankMasterFields
Name Hide bank master data fields  in Bank Account questions
Default value No
Bank Account  questions, which collect supplier bank information, include a set of predefined  fields  that are
visible by default. This parameter controls whether some of those fields  are hidden, and therefore uneditable, in
questionnaires in your site. When set to Y es, it hides the following fields:
•Name
•Bank Branch
•Street
•City
•State/Province/Region
•Postal Code
These fields  are considered bank master data and are typically maintained with bank master records in an
integrated ERP system. Hiding these fields  avoids confusion for questionnaire recipients who don't know these
details and prevents them from entering answers that conflict  with the ERP bank master records. This parameter's
default setting, No, means that the predefined  bank master data fields  in Bank Account  questions are visible and
questionnaire recipients can edit them.
These fields  are visible in the ERP data  detail area of the supplier profile  even when hidden in questionnaires.
If you have existing suppliers with Bank Account  data, when you enable this parameter, questionnaire recipients
can no longer see bank master data they previously added.
A member of the Customer Administrator  group sets this parameter in the Intelligent Configuration  Manager
workspace.

Hide timestamps in external supplier request names in
project search
Hides the timestamps in the names of external supplier requests in project search results. These timestamps
reflect  the server time zone rather than the time zone where the request was submitted.
IDApplication.SM.ExcludeDateInSupplierRequestTitle
Name Hide timestamps in external supplier request names in project search
Default value No
By default, when you search for external supplier request projects using project search on the Home  dashboard,
search results include a "submitted on" timestamp in the supplier request project name. Other types of supplier
management projects, such as registrations and modular questionnaires, don't include this timestamp in project
search results. The timestamp in search results reflects  the time the external supplier request was submitted in the
server time zone, while the project history for the external supplier request shows the timestamp in the time zone
of the submitting supplier. If you don't find  the timestamps in project search results useful, or if they confuse users
in your site, you can hide them by setting this parameter to Y es.
 Note
Setting this parameter to Y es hides timestamps in the names of external supplier requests created after the
parameter is enabled. It doesn't hide timestamps in the names of external supplier requests created before the
parameter was enabled.
A member of the Customer Administrator  group sets this parameter in the Intelligent Configuration  Manager
workspace.

through vendor.vendorInfo.name4  from 35 to 40 characters each, and the setting for

Field Restrictions  validation tool. Keep this parameter enabled if name fields  in an integrated ERP system also
have 40 character maximum lengths (160 total), or disable it if the ERP system’s name fields  have a 35 character
maximum length (140 total).
IDApplication.SM.Enable160CharacterSupportForFullName
Name Increase maximum size for name fields  to 40 characters each in applied field  restrictions
Default value No
The Apply Field Restrictions  validation tool on pages for adding or editing questions in supplier management
project templates validates field  mappings and suggests values for ERP-related settings such as maximum length.
By default, it sets a maximum length of 35 characters for individual name fields  and 140 characters for the full
name field.  These default settings are appropriate in sites integrated with SAP ECC, where name fields  have the
same maximum lengths.
Enable this parameter to increase maximum length settings to 40 for individual name fields  and 160 for the full
name field  if your site meets the following conditions:
•The supplier full name feature (SM-14213) is enabled. It's disabled by default.
•Name fields  in an integrated ERP system such as SAP S/4HANA or SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) have
a maximum length of 40 characters.
•The support for supplier names of up to 160 characters feature (SM-36076) is enabled. It's
disabled by default and its availability is controlled by the self-service configuration  parameter
Application.SM.Enable160CharacterSupportForFullName  in

mappings in supplier management questionnaires  in Intelligent Configuration  Manager .

overrides existing supplier source systems of SM with SAP for any suppliers with organization IDs in the SAP
domain. By default, the vendor push task doesn’t update source systems once set. Disable this parameter if your

Default value Y es

toolkit file transfer to add suppliers to their site and then use the vendor push task enabled by the site configuration
parameter Application.SM.VendorContactPushTaskEnabled  (set by SAP Ariba Support) to push that data
to the supplier management database. If your supplier data load process adds suppliers first  and adds organization
IDs for suppliers later, depending on timing, the vendor push task can add the new suppliers to the supplier
management database before their organization IDs are added. In this situation, the suppliers are added with an
SM source system. The default setting for Override existing source system with SAP domain values during
vendor push  means that when you update those suppliers to add organization IDs in the SAP domain, the vendor
push task updates their source systems from SM to SAP in the supplier management database.

synchronization if the field  is blank or empty in the corresponding supplier management record. By default,
synchronization doesn’t remove DUNS IDs from supplier organizations even if the corresponding supplier
management field  is blank or empty.
IDApplication.SM.ResetDunsInVendorPullTaskIfValueIsEmpty
Name Remove blank DUNS IDs in supplier data synchronization from supplier management to sourcing
Default value No
Enable this parameter to ensure that when a D-U-N-S number is removed from the supplier record through
removal of an answer to a supplier management questionnaire, ERP data synchronization, or a data import in SM
Administration , the new blank value is synchronized to the supplier organization and common supplier. Otherwise,
the supplier organization and common supplier records retain the previous value.

Specifies  that Attachment  and Expiration Date are the only two required fields  for answers to certificate  questions
in external supplier management questionnaires. Otherwise, all certificate  fields  are required.

Name Require only attachment and expiration date for supplier certificates
Default value False
The default value of False  means that all the certificate  fields  are mandatory and the supplier must enter values
in all the fields.  The certificate  detail fields  are required if a supplier answers Y es to a certificate  question. The
certificate  detail fields  are not required if a supplier answers No to a certificate  question.

Set maximum length for Room ID in Extended Address
Specifies  the maximum length for the Room  ID field  in Extended Address  questions. The default setting, 10, is the
required setting for answers in mapped questions in supplier management questionnaires, although the base field
supports up to 20 characters.
IDApplication.SM.RoomIDFieldMaxLength
Name Set maximum length for Room ID in Extended Address
Default value10
Extended Address  questions include a set of predefined  answer fields,  including a Room  field.  The corresponding
mapped vendor.addressExt.postalAddress.roomID  field  in the supplier database has a maximum field
length of 10 characters. However, the question field  allows answers of up to 20 characters. The default setting
of this parameter, 10, ensures that Room  answers in mapped Extended Address  questions synchronize to the
database without causing errors. Since those errors can prevent successful submission of questionnaires such as
the supplier request, SAP Ariba recommends against changing this default setting.
A member of the Customer Administrator  group sets this parameter in the Intelligent Configuration  Manager
workspace.

Set minimum registration status for event participation
Specifies  the minimum registration status that a supplier must have to participate in sourcing events. Valid values
are 0 for Not Invited , 1 for Invited , 2 for Pending Approval , and 3 for Registered .
IDApplication.SM.MinimumRegistrationStatusForEventAccess
Name Set minimum registration status for event participation
Default value0
Suppliers who are below the minimum status can be invited to events, but can't access them until they've achieved
the minimum status. The restriction applies to suppliers who have been qualified  for the event commodities,
regions, and departments using qualification  data import but who haven't yet achieved the required registration
status.
The default value is 0. The valid values are:
Value Description Associated Registration Invitations
0 or Not Invited Suppliers who haven't yet been invited
to register can participate in sourcing
events.There are no automatic registration invi-
tations.
1 or InvitedSuppliers must have already been invited
to register to participate in sourcing
events. However, they don't need to sub-
mit registration questionnaires to partici-
pate in events.If the person who invites a Not
Invited  supplier to participate in an
event is a member of both the
Event Administrator  and Supplier
Registration Manager  groups, the sup-
plier is invited to register at the same
time automatically, and their registration
status changes to Invited . Otherwise, the
supplier must be invited to register sepa-
rately.2 or Pending ApprovalSuppliers whose registration is pending
approval or who are already registered
can participate in sourcing events. They
must submit registration questionnaires
to participate in events, but their registra-
tion doesn't need to be approved.
3 or Registered Only suppliers with approved registra-
tions can participate in sourcing events.
They must submit registration question-
naires and their registrations must be
approved before they can participate in
events.
 Note
•Event templates have a supplier eligibility rule based on registration status. That rule governs whether or
not suppliers can be invited  to an event. This parameter governs their ability to participate  after invitation.
•Changes to this parameter's setting apply only to participants invited to events after they're made.
This parameter is only applicable in sites that

For supplier bank accounts collected in Bank Account  questions, SAP Ariba automatically generates unique,
sequential IDs. Automatically generated bank IDs consist of sequential numerical values and, optionally, a country/
region-specific  prefix.  By default, these automatically generated bank IDs start with 0000. If you set this parameter

Validate required questions in repeatable sections in external
questionnaires
Validates required questions in repeatable sections in external questionnaires so that if there's no prepopulated
answer in the initial questionnaire draft, suppliers must provide answers before submitting.
IDApplication.SM.ValidationOfRequiredQuesInRSEnabled
Name Validate required questions in repeatable sections in external questionnaires
Default value No
By default, when a repeatable section in an external questionnaire contains an individual required question,
there's no validation check to ensure that the recipient has provided the required answer before submitting the
questionnaire. Enabling this parameter applies a validation check to ensure that individual required questions
within repeatable sections in external questionnaires are also validated so that recipients must provide answers
before submitting the questionnaire.
This setting only applies to individual required questions in external questionnaires edited by suppliers on SAP
Business Network. It doesn't apply to internal questionnaires, or to external questionnaires edited by buyer users
on behalf of suppliers.
A member of the Customer Administrator  group sets this parameter in the Intelligent Configuration  Manager
workspace.

Managing Configuration  Parameters in Supplier
Administration
The configuration  parameters in Supplier Administration control some specific  supplier management functionality.
Administrators can modify the values for these configuration  parameters without having to raise a case with SAP
Ariba Support. Use this procedure to manage configuration  parameters.
Prerequisites
T o manage parameters in SM Administration , you must be a member of the SM Ops Administrator  group.
Managing Suppliers and Supplier Lifecycles
Site Configuration  Parameters for Supplier Management PUBLIC 353

Context
Y ou modify parameter values to enable or disable functionality controlled by the configuration  parameters.
For more information about specific  parameters, refer to Reference of Configuration  Parameters in Supplier
Administration [page 318] .
Procedure
1. On the dashboard, choose 
 Manage
 SM Administration
 .
2. Choose Configuration  Parameters .
3. Choose the options icon (  ) to expand the list of options.
4. Do one of the following:
•T o see the parameter's description or modify its current value, choose Edit . If you're modifying a value,
choose or enter the new value and choose Save .
•T o restore the parameter's default value, choose Reset .

Procurement Contract Requirement Principles
A practical 10-page guide for deciding when a purchase order is sufficient and when a formal written contract should be used.
Purpose
- Provide a category-based rule set that procurement, sourcing, and business stakeholders can apply before engaging a supplier.
- Use the UNSPSC hierarchy as the organizing structure so decisions can be mapped to spend categories instead of being made ad hoc.
- Summarize public best practices on contract need, supplier risk, and situations where a purchase order alone is usually not enough.
Scope
- This document is written for commercial and institutional procurement teams in the United States.
- It addresses goods, services, software, data handling, project work, recurring arrangements, and higher-risk supplier relationships.
- It is a governance and procurement practice document, not legal advice. Local law, regulated-industry requirements, and internal delegated authority rules still control.
Prepared on April 13, 2026.
Core Rule: PO First, Contract When Risk or Complexity Requires
Working principle
- A purchase order is generally sufficient for straightforward, one-time purchases where the item, quantity, price, and delivery terms are clear and the supplier accepts the buyer's ordering terms.
- A formal written contract is generally needed when the transaction is service-heavy, longer term, legally complex, negotiable, high value, or exposed to operational, regulatory, privacy, safety, or reputational risk.
PO is usually sufficient when
- The purchase is for standard goods or catalog items, the relationship ends when delivery is complete, and no bespoke legal provisions are required.
- The commercial terms are stable and can be handled through standard PO terms and conditions.
Formal contract is usually needed when
- The deal involves services, project milestones, intellectual property, confidentiality, data access, supplier performance commitments, insurance, indemnities, or non-standard liability allocation.
- The supplier asks for a signature, presents its own paper, or the arrangement extends beyond a simple one-time order.
How UNSPSC Should Drive the Decision
Use the hierarchy
- UNSPSC classifies spend using segment, family, class, and commodity. The contract decision should usually begin at segment or family level, then tighten at class or commodity level if the work is

ndard liability allocation.
- The supplier asks for a signature, presents its own paper, or the arrangement extends beyond a simple one-time order.
How UNSPSC Should Drive the Decision
Use the hierarchy
- UNSPSC classifies spend using segment, family, class, and commodity. The contract decision should usually begin at segment or family level, then tighten at class or commodity level if the work is specialized.
- Lower-risk commodity families often support PO-first buying. Higher-risk service or regulated families should trigger contract review by default.
Examples of category logic
- Routine office supplies within Office Equipment and Accessories families are commonly PO-sufficient.
- Software, consulting, engineering, laboratory services, and outsourced technical support often require written agreements even if the dollar amount is modest.
- The same broad segment can contain both PO-sufficient and contract-required buys, so the family and class matter.
Governance rule
- Treat UNSPSC as the starting classifier for workflow routing, but never as the only decision factor. Risk, law, data, and supplier terms can override category defaults.
UNSPSC Categories Commonly Suitable for PO-Only Buying
Typical PO-first categories
- Routine goods in standard supply families such as office supplies, janitorial supplies, basic maintenance materials, low-complexity tools, commodity packaging, and standard replacement parts.
- Catalog or market-standard items where specifications are objective, delivery is discrete, and acceptance depends mainly on quantity, condition, and conformance.
- Low-risk spot buys with no access to systems, facilities, people, confidential information, or regulated materials.
Conditions that should still be true
- No ongoing statement of work is required.
- No intellectual property is being developed, assigned, licensed, or restricted.
- No personal data, health data, payment card data, or sensitive research information is shared with the supplier.
- The supplier is not performing work on-site under safety or labor controls that require allocated responsibilities.
Practical view
- If the transaction can be fully described by item, price, quantity, shipment, inspection, and payment terms, a PO is often enough.
UNSPSC Categories That Should Default to Formal Contract Review
High-likelihood contract categories
- Professional, consulting, technical,

e supplier is not performing work on-site under safety or labor controls that require allocated responsibilities.
Practical view
- If the transaction can be fully described by item, price, quantity, shipment, inspection, and payment terms, a PO is often enough.
UNSPSC Categories That Should Default to Formal Contract Review
High-likelihood contract categories
- Professional, consulting, technical, and managed services.
- Software, SaaS, cloud, data access, hosting, maintenance, and integration services.
- Construction, engineering, installation, and facility work involving performance, safety, permits, or site access.
- Research, laboratory, clinical, regulated, or specialized scientific services.
- Independent contractor engagements and work performed by non-employees where law or classification rules may require written terms.
Why these categories need contracts
- They typically require scope definition, deliverables, acceptance criteria, project governance, service levels, term and termination rights, liability language, and documented compliance obligations.
- A PO usually does not capture enough detail for disputes over work quality, timing, change control, or legal responsibility.
Risk Triggers That Override Category Defaults
A contract should be required regardless of UNSPSC category if any of the following apply
- The supplier will receive, host, process, or access confidential information, employee data, customer data, student data, health information, cardholder data, trade secrets, or research information.
- The supplier will develop deliverables, software, content, designs, or inventions where ownership, licensing, or use rights must be clear.
- The relationship is ongoing, auto-renewing, subscription-based, exclusive, or difficult to exit without transition obligations.
- The supplier is entering facilities, using hazardous materials, handling regulated goods, or performing safety-critical work.
- The transaction includes non-standard payment terms, advance payments, milestone payments, credits, rebates, or penalties.
- The supplier's paper includes limitations of liability, disclaimers, unilateral amendments, automatic renewals, or venue and governing-law clauses that need review.
Bottom line
- If the business needs terms beyond price and delivery, it probably needs a contract.
Category-by-Category Principles Using UNSPSC

dard payment terms, advance payments, milestone payments, credits, rebates, or penalties.
- The supplier's paper includes limitations of liability, disclaimers, unilateral amendments, automatic renewals, or venue and governing-law clauses that need review.
Bottom line
- If the business needs terms beyond price and delivery, it probably needs a contract.
Category-by-Category Principles Using UNSPSC Families
Goods-oriented families often suitable for PO-first treatment
- Office Equipment and Accessories and Supplies, standard cleaning supplies, standard tools, common packaging, and commodity maintenance materials can normally start with PO-only rules.
Goods-oriented families that often still need contracts
- Capital equipment with installation or maintenance obligations, chemicals or gases outside standard catalog channels, leased equipment, and goods tied to warranties, calibration, training, or service obligations should move into contract review.
Service-oriented families that should default to contract review
- Consulting, IT and telecom services, software and licensing, engineering, temporary labor, facilities services, logistics outsourcing, marketing services, and scientific services.
Regulated or sensitive families
- Medical, laboratory, clinical, research, payment-processing, and information-management families should be presumed contract-required because legal and operational controls usually extend beyond PO terms.
When Local Law, Jurisdiction, and Compliance Make Contracting Mandatory
Legal and compliance triggers
- Sale-of-goods rules in the United States can support PO-based contracting for goods, but service arrangements often need fuller written terms because scope, deliverables, and liability are not adequately covered by basic order language.
- Independent contractor rules, labor classification rules, privacy laws, export controls, healthcare or education privacy requirements, and regulated-industry obligations may require specific written clauses.
- International services or work performed outside the United States can create tax, sanctions, currency, local-law, and enforcement issues that are difficult to handle with PO terms alone.
Approval principle
- Any transaction touching local law, public sector rules, regulated data, or mandatory compliance flow-downs should be routed to formal contract review before work begins.
Practical

clauses.
- International services or work performed outside the United States can create tax, sanctions, currency, local-law, and enforcement issues that are difficult to handle with PO terms alone.
Approval principle
- Any transaction touching local law, public sector rules, regulated data, or mandatory compliance flow-downs should be routed to formal contract review before work begins.
Practical implication
- The more the transaction depends on jurisdiction-specific rules, the less appropriate PO-only buying becomes.
Decision Process and Operating Checklist
Step 1
- Classify the spend using UNSPSC segment, family, and class.
Step 2
- Ask whether the supplier is selling a standard good, providing a service, enabling software or data access, or entering a continuing relationship.
Step 3
- Check for risk triggers: personal or confidential data, IP, safety, insurance, site access, regulatory controls, auto-renewal, milestone payments, or supplier paper requiring signature.
Step 4
- If no trigger exists and the buy is a simple one-time goods purchase, issue a PO under standard terms.
Step 5
- If any trigger exists, use a written contract and attach a PO only as the ordering or payment instrument if internal process requires it.
Operating principle
- Never let a supplier begin work, access data, or reserve capacity before the required contract and approvals are fully in place.
Recommended Policy Statement and Public Sources
Model policy statement
- A purchase order may be used as the primary purchasing instrument for routine, low-risk, one-time goods purchases where the full commercial understanding is captured by item description, quantity, price, delivery, and standard terms.
- A formal written contract is required for services, software, data access, independent contractors, ongoing relationships, supplier paper requiring signature, and transactions involving elevated legal, regulatory, operational, or reputational risk.
Public sources used
- U.S. Department of Commerce: UNSPSC overview and hierarchy.
- Cornell University Legal Information Institute: UCC Article 2 on sale of goods.
- University System of New Hampshire Procurement: PO vs. Contract guidance.
- Stanford Procurement Contracts: contract triggers, services, independent contractors, software, data security, and master agreement concepts.
- UC Santa Barbara Procurement: data security, privacy, and

mmerce: UNSPSC overview and hierarchy.
- Cornell University Legal Information Institute: UCC Article 2 on sale of goods.
- University System of New Hampshire Procurement: PO vs. Contract guidance.
- Stanford Procurement Contracts: contract triggers, services, independent contractors, software, data security, and master agreement concepts.
- UC Santa Barbara Procurement: data security, privacy, and confidentiality as contract triggers.
Use note
- Adopt this document as a decision framework, then add your organization's signature authority, review thresholds, approved templates, and local legal requirements.

Purpose of These Guidelines
Section 01  |  Purpose & Scope
Why This Document Exists
These Procurement Guidelines establish a consistent, transparent and accountable framework for all purchasing activity at [Company Name]. They ensure that public and organisational funds are spent wisely, suppliers are treated fairly, and decisions can withstand scrutiny.

Core Objectives
• Achieve best value for money across all spend categories
• Ensure compliance with applicable laws, regulations and internal policy
• Promote fair and open competition among qualified suppliers
• Mitigate financial, legal, reputational and operational risk
• Support sustainability, diversity and ethical supply chain practices
• Provide clear guidance to staff with purchasing responsibilities

In Scope
All employees, contractors and agents who commit or authorise expenditure on behalf of [Company Name]
All categories of spend: goods, services, software, works and consulting
All procurement methods from petty cash to formal tendering
All business units, subsidiaries and project entities where [Company Name] controls the purchasing decision
Exclusions / Special Rules
Regulated procurement governed by specific legislation (supplements, does not replace, those rules)
Capital investment decisions subject to separate Board approval thresholds
Emergency procurement — abbreviated rules apply (see Section 09)
Inter-company transactions between wholly-owned entities — subject to Transfer Pricing policy

SECTION 02
Governance & Authority

[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines

Core Procurement Principles
Section 02  |  Governance & Authority
Value for Money
Select suppliers on total cost of ownership, quality, delivery and risk — not lowest price alone.
Transparency
Decisions must be documented, auditable and based on pre-defined criteria disclosed to all bidders.
Fairness
All qualified suppliers must receive equal information, equal time and equal consideration.
Accountability
Every purchase must be authorised at the correct level; approvers are personally responsible for compliance.
Integrity
No conflicts of interest, gifts, or improper inducements. Violations are treated as gross misconduct.
Sustainability
Preference to suppliers with credible environmental, social and governance (ESG) commitments.

Supplier Qualification & Registration
Section 03  |  Supplier Management
STEP 1
Expression of Interest
Supplier submits company profile, certifications, financial statements and references via the supplier portal.
STEP 2
Due Diligence Review
Procurement screens for sanctions, debarment, financial health, insurance, tax compliance and ESG standing.
STEP 3
Capability Assessment
Technical evaluation against category-specific criteria; site visits or demonstrations may be required.
STEP 4
Approval & Onboarding
Approved suppliers are added to the preferred supplier list (PSL) and issued with a Supplier Code.
STEP 5
Annual Renewal
Suppliers must reconfirm key data annually. Failure to renew suspends the supplier from active use.

Quality

Quarterly

Quarterly

Quarterly

Preferred Supplier List (PSL) Policy
Section 03  |  Supplier Management
Buy from PSL first:  For all spend categories where a PSL exists, buyers must source from approved PSL suppliers before approaching the open market.
PSL waivers:  A PSL bypass requires written justification and CPO approval. Recurring bypasses trigger PSL review.
PSL reviews:  Each category PSL is reviewed annually. Underperforming suppliers are placed on probation or removed.
New entrants:  Suppliers not on the PSL may be added following successful qualification (see Slide 11). Emergency entrants require post-event evaluation.
Market testing:  Even within PSL categories, competitive quotes must be obtained above $[B] to confirm market alignment.
Transparency:  All PSL additions, removals and waivers are logged and reported quarterly to the Procurement Committee.

RFP / RFQ Process Steps
Section 04  |  Sourcing Methods
01
Define Requirements
Business need, specs, budget, timeline, evaluation criteria.
02
Approve Sourcing Plan
Procurement validates method; budget holder confirms funding.
03
Identify Suppliers
PSL check; market scan; shortlist minimum 3 qualified suppliers.
04
Issue RFQ / RFP
Distribute package; Q&A period; all clarifications shared equally.
05
Evaluate Responses
Score against pre-set criteria; involve cross-functional panel.
06
Select & Negotiate
Preferred supplier selected; BAFO if applicable; terms agreed.
07
Award & Notify
Approved award letter; unsuccessful suppliers notified promptly.
08
Contract & Onboard
Signed contract or PO; supplier onboarded; KPIs activated.
[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines  |  CONFIDENTIAL
16

Evaluation Criteria & Award Decisions
Section 04  |  Sourcing Methods
Criteria must be pre-defined:  Weightings and scoring rubric must be agreed before issuing the RFQ/RFP. Post-submission changes are not permitted.
Typical weighting (services):  Technical / capability 40% · Commercial / price 40% · Implementation plan 10% · ESG / risk 10%.
Typical weighting (goods):  Price / TCO 50% · Quality / spec compliance 30% · Delivery & reliability 10% · ESG / risk 10%.
Evaluation panel:  Minimum two evaluators; at least one from Procurement. Panel members must sign conflict-of-interest declarations.
Award rationale:  Written recommendation required for all awards above $[B]. The record must explain why the recommended supplier offers best value.
Unsuccessful bidder feedback:  Suppliers may request a debrief within 10 business days of notification. Procurement must provide meaningful feedback.

[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines  |  CONFIDENTIAL

SECTION 05
Contract Management
Ensuring every agreement is documented, performance-managed and renewed or exited in an orderly way.
[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines

Purchase Order vs. Formal Contract
Section 05  |  Contract Management
USE A PURCHASE ORDER WHEN…
One-time purchase of standard goods or catalogue items
Full commercial terms captured by item, price, quantity and delivery
No IP, confidential data, site access or performance obligation
Supplier accepts standard PO terms and conditions
Value below $[C] with no ongoing relationship
USE A FORMAL CONTRACT WHEN…
Services, project milestones or any ongoing engagement
Software, SaaS, data access, hosting or system integration
Supplier handles confidential, personal or regulated data
IP ownership, licensing or use rights must be defined
Value above $[C] OR supplier presents their own paper
[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines  |  CONFIDENTIAL
19

Contract Lifecycle Management
Section 05  |  Contract Management
Contract repository:  All signed contracts must be uploaded to the central contracts register within 5 business days of execution.
Obligation tracking:  Key dates (expiry, renewal, break clauses, price reviews) are flagged in the system with 90-day advance alerts.
Variation management:  Any change to scope, price or term requires a signed contract amendment. Verbal agreements are not binding.
Performance reviews:  Contracts above $[B] p.a. require a minimum quarterly performance review with documented outcomes.
Renewal process:  Renewals must be considered at least 90 days before expiry. Auto-renewal clauses require CPO sign-off to activate.
Exit & handover:  Exit plans are required for all critical supplier contracts. Transition and knowledge-transfer obligations must be specified in the contract.

SECTION 06
Ethics & Compliance
Maintaining the highest standards of integrity, preventing corruption and managing conflicts of interest.
[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines

Ethical Standards & Conflict of Interest
Section 06  |  Ethics & Compliance
Zero tolerance on corruption:  Bribery, kickbacks, facilitation payments and improper gifts are strictly prohibited under all circumstances.
Gifts & hospitality:  Gifts above $[gift threshold] must be declared. Hospitality from suppliers requires line-manager approval before acceptance.
Conflict of interest:  Any personal or financial interest in a supplier must be declared in writing before that procurement begins. Affected individuals must recuse themselves entirely.
Confidentiality:  Pricing, bid information and supplier data are confidential. Sharing with unauthorised parties is a disciplinary matter.
Supplier entertainment:  Staff may not solicit meals, travel or entertainment from suppliers. Unsolicited events must be declared.
Whistleblowing:  Suspected misconduct should be reported via the confidential ethics hotline. Reporters are protected from retaliation.
[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines  |  CONFIDENTIAL

[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines  |  CONFIDENTIAL
22

Sustainable & Responsible Procurement
Section 06  |  Ethics & Compliance
Environmental criteria:  Evaluation weightings must include carbon footprint, packaging, and end-of-life disposal for relevant categories.
Social value:  Preference for suppliers with demonstrable commitments to local employment, living wages and workforce diversity.
Modern slavery:  All suppliers above $[D] must complete a Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Declaration annually.
Supplier diversity:  [Company Name] targets [X]% of addressable spend with certified diverse, minority-owned or SME businesses by [year].
Circular economy:  Where technically feasible, preference is given to reconditioned, remanufactured or recycled goods over new production.
Scope 3 emissions:  Strategic suppliers are required to disclose Scope 1 & 2 emissions and set reduction targets aligned with [Company Name]'s climate commitments.

eProcurement Systems & Data Management
Section 08  |  Technology & Reporting
All purchase requisitions must be raised in the approved eProcurement system — email and verbal orders are non-compliant
Supplier master data is maintained by Procurement only; requesters may not create vendor records
PO numbers must be obtained before goods or services are ordered; retrospective POs require CPO approval
The system is the system of record; paper processes require explicit exception approval and must be entered within 2 days
System access is role-based; users are responsible for all transactions made under their login
Catalogue items should be purchased from within the system catalogue; off-catalogue requests require category team approval
Spend data is classified to UNSPSC commodity level for all transactions above $[A]
Monthly spend reports are distributed to department heads and category managers
The Procurement Dashboard tracks savings, compliance rate, PSL usage, and supplier diversity spend
All sourcing documents (RFPs, evaluations, award rationale) are retained for a minimum of 7 years
Data quality reviews are conducted quarterly; category managers are accountable for their data
Third-party spend analytics tools may be used; data sharing must comply with the Data Governance Policy

SECTION 09
Exceptions & Waivers
How [Company Name] handles emergencies, sole-source justifications and legitimate policy departures.
[Company Name]  |  Procurement Guidelines

Exceptions, Waivers & Emergency Procurement
Section 09  |  Exceptions & Waivers
Waiver definition:  A waiver permits departure from one or more procurement requirements. It does not waive the need for authorisation or documentation.
Who can approve waivers:  Value-dependent. Waivers below $[C] require CPO sign-off. Above $[C] require CFO + CPO. No self-approval is permitted.
Sole-source justification:  Must demonstrate: only one capable supplier exists OR switching costs make competition impractical. Price reasonableness evidence is always required.
Emergency procurement:  Applies when a delay would cause material harm to operations, safety or continuity. The minimum viable process must still be followed. Full retrospective documentation required within 5 days.
Retrospective POs:  Goods or services received without a PO require a retrospective PO and a written explanation. Third occurrence by the same requester triggers a formal warning.
Waiver register:  All approved waivers are logged and reported quarterly to the Audit Committee. Patterns of excessive waivers trigger a policy review.

When to Engage Procurement
30-slide quick guide for business teams and requesters
Built from public procurement planning and compliance guidance
Focus: when Procurement should be brought in early, not after the fact
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 1

Why Early Engagement Matters
Reduces rework, delays, and non-compliant buying
Improves sourcing strategy, pricing leverage, and supplier competition
Helps define scope, route approvals, and avoid rushed exceptions
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 2

Simple Rule
If the request is more than a basic low-risk catalog buy, engage Procurement early
The more complex, regulated, high-value, or strategic the purchase, the earlier Procurement should be involved
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 3

Engage Procurement At The Idea Stage
New project or new supplier
Budget is being shaped
Requirements are still being defined
Stakeholders are deciding buy, build, renew, or extend
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 4

Engage Before Contacting Suppliers
Before requesting pricing outside approved channels
Before promising business or asking for a proposal
Before receiving supplier paper that may create a contracting issue
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 5

Engage For New Suppliers
Supplier onboarding is needed
Tax, banking, sanctions, or due diligence checks are required
Business wants to move quickly but supplier setup is not complete
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 6

Engage For Higher Value Buys
Larger purchases usually require more quotes, formal competition, or documented exceptions
If internal thresholds may be crossed, Procurement should confirm the correct process before the request advances
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 7

Engage For Competitive Events
When the business wants multiple bids
When evaluation criteria must be documented
When fairness, transparency, or auditability matter
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 8

Engage For Services
Consulting, staffing, engineering, training, maintenance, logistics, managed services
Services need scope, deliverables, milestones, and acceptance criteria
A PO alone is often not enough
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 9

Engage For Software And SaaS
Software license, subscription, cloud, hosting, support, or implementation
Procurement should align commercial terms with Legal, Security, Privacy, and IT
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 10

Engage For Data Or Privacy Risk
Supplier will access confidential, customer, employee, financial, health, or research data
Security, privacy, breach, and data-processing terms must be handled before award
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 11

Engage For Contracting
Supplier asks for signature
Supplier sends terms and conditions, MSA, SOW, order form, or quote with legal language
Business wants to start work before terms are finalized
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 12

Engage For Renewals
Contract expiry is approaching
Auto-renewal exists
Price increase, scope change, or new stakeholder needs must be reviewed before renewal
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 13

Engage For Sole Source
Only one supplier appears viable
Business wants to waive competition
Justification and exception workflow may be required
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 14

Engage For Urgent Requests
Procurement should still be engaged even when timing is tight
Urgency is a planning signal, not a reason to bypass controls
Early escalation gives the best chance of compliant fast-tracking
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 15

Engage For Scope Changes
Existing supplier is adding new work
Volume, term, price, geography, or deliverables are changing
Change orders and amendments may need Procurement review
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 16

Engage For Risky Supplier Terms
Auto-renewal
Unlimited use restrictions or unfavorable IP terms
Broad disclaimers, weak SLAs, or restrictive liability caps
Advance payment or unusual commercial structure
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 17

Engage For Regulated Or Safety Critical Purchases
Healthcare, lab, construction, hazardous materials, site work, export controls, or regulated categories
Procurement helps coordinate the right review path and required clauses
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 18

Engage For Cross Functional Review
Legal for contract language
InfoSec or Privacy for data handling
Finance for budget and payment structure
Operations for implementation impact
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 19

When Procurement May Not Need Full Involvement
Low-value approved catalog buy
Existing approved contract and supplier
Clear scope, standard terms, and no new risk trigger
Even then, follow policy and threshold rules
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 20

What Procurement Typically Needs From The Business
Business need and outcome
Budget and timing
Scope or specification
Preferred supplier status if any
Key stakeholders and approval owner
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 21

Good Intake Questions
What are we buying and why now?
Is this goods, services, software, or a mixed deal?
Will the supplier access data or facilities?
Do we need competition, a contract, or an exception?
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 22

Common Late Engagement Problems
Supplier already selected before process review
Quotes not comparable
Budget approved without total cost visibility
Contract review starts after work is expected to begin
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 23

Red Flags
Business says we just need a PO but the supplier sent a contract
Requester says work starts next week
Supplier will touch personal or confidential data
No one can explain the sourcing rationale or evaluation basis
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 24

Practical Trigger Matrix
Low complexity + low value + standard goods = often PO route
Medium complexity or value = involve Procurement for route check
High complexity, high value, services, software, or risk = engage Procurement immediately
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 25

Suggested Timeline
30 to 60 days: simple sourcing with low legal complexity
60 to 120 days: competitive events, new suppliers, or negotiated contracts
120+ days: strategic, regulated, global, or highly integrated procurements
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 26

Role Of Procurement
Guide the sourcing process
Protect commercial position
Improve competition and supplier quality
Coordinate governance without slowing the business unnecessarily
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 27

What Requesters Should Do
Plan earlier than feels necessary
Bring Procurement in before supplier commitment
Share full context, not just the quote
Raise urgency and exceptions openly
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 28

Bottom Line
Engage Procurement when the purchase is strategic, complex, high-value, service-based, data-sensitive, contractual, or time-critical
If there is any doubt, engage early and let Procurement route it correctly
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 29

Public Guidance Used
US EPA procurement best practices on starting early
University of Kansas Procurement compliance guidance on thresholds and quotes
GOV.UK and WA Government guidance on planning and early market engagement
Deck adapted into a new internal-use summary
Procurement engagement guide | Slide 30

Managing Projects, Teams, Documents, and
Tasks
This guide is for SAP Ariba users and administrators who need to create and manage projects, project teams,
project documents, and tasks in SAP Ariba Strategic Sourcing and Supplier Management solutions. This guide also
describes how to export project data to files.
This guide applies to:
•SAP Ariba Strategic Sourcing Suite
•SAP Ariba Sourcing
•SAP Ariba Contracts
•SAP Ariba Supplier Information and Performance Management
•SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance
•SAP Ariba Supplier Risk
 Note
As of May 2025, new subscriptions to SAP Ariba Strategic Sourcing Suite are no longer available.

10 PUBLICManaging Projects, T eams, Documents, and T asks
Managing Projects, Teams, Documents, and Tasks

What Is a Project?
A project is a container that enables you to maintain all documents and information for an item such as an SAP
Ariba Sourcing event, an SAP Ariba Contracts contract, or a procurement project.
Projects enable multiple users to work on the same documents or events with defined  roles and processes. With
projects, you can specify what tasks must be completed, who is responsible for completing a task, and when a task
must be completed.
Project Types
The types of projects you can create vary depending on your SAP Ariba solution.
SAP Ariba Sourcing Project Types
SAP Ariba Sourcing supports three types of sourcing projects:
•Quick Projects
A quick project is a single sourcing event such as an RFI, RFP , or auction that is created from a default SAP
Ariba template. Quick projects are limited to the functionality defined  in those templates, and are useful for
creating basic events that do not require a lot of customization or process management.
•Full Projects
A full project can be created from any available template, including templates your company has created to
manage different  business processes or types of events. Full projects can contain multiple sourcing events, or
they can be sourcing projects. They include full support for process management, including document storage,
tasks, a message board, and project team. It is common for companies use a sourcing project that includes
planning and savings tracking to manage one or more subproject events.
Events have specific  features that are described in the Event Management Guide . The topics in this collection
describe the features that are available in full sourcing projects.
•Sourcing Requests
A Sourcing Request  is a project type used to request a sourcing project. In most cases, a sourcing request
contains the following items:
•a sourcing project as a subproject of the request
•an approval task; the subproject becomes visible in the Documents  area of the sourcing request after the
approval task is approved
•a special Sourcing Request Items  document, which can contain event information (rules, team, suppliers,
and content)
The subproject is a placeholder that the sourcing manager uses to create a sourcing event or project.
Information in the Sourcing Request Items  document is propagated to the sourcing project (or event) created
from the sourcing request.
There are two types of

•a special Sourcing Request Items  document, which can contain event information (rules, team, suppliers,
and content)
The subproject is a placeholder that the sourcing manager uses to create a sourcing event or project.
Information in the Sourcing Request Items  document is propagated to the sourcing project (or event) created

There are two types of sourcing requests:
Managing Projects, T eams, Documents, and T asks
What Is a Project? PUBLIC 11

•sourcing requests created from integration with external systems
In sites with the RFQ and Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature, a sourcing request can be
created automatically in SAP Ariba Sourcing based on the RFQ information received from the SAP or
Oracle ERP system. No manual tasks are needed to create the sourcing request after the integration is
configured.  RFQ information (including line item data) is stored in the Sourcing Request Items  document.
Sourcing managers can review and approve the sourcing request and can create a sourcing event from the
sourcing request.
SAP Ariba Sourcing provides a default template, Sourcing Request T emplate  for sourcing requests
created from integration with external systems. A template author can create a new version of this
template and modify it as needed.
•sourcing requests created from the dashboard
Any user can create a sourcing request from the dashboard regardless of their group membership. Users
can record their purchase requirements in the Sourcing Request Items  document. After a sourcing
request has been approved, the sourcing manager or any other user who has the permission to create a
sourcing project can create a sourcing project, based on the information in the sourcing request.
SAP Ariba Sourcing does not provide a default template for creating a sourcing request from the
dashboard. A template author can create a sourcing request template by creating a copy of the Sourcing
Request T emplate  template provided for integration and modifying the copy. For more information, see
Project T emplate Guide .
SAP Ariba Contracts Project Types
SAP Ariba Contracts supports two types of contract projects:
•Contract Workspaces
A Contract Workspace  is project used for contracts and includes information about the contract, contract
documents, project team members, and tasks.
•Contract Request s
A Contract Request  is a project used to request a contract workspace. Contract requests usually contain a
subset of the information used in a contract workspace and include the ability to create a contract workspace
from the information provided. Contract requests are often used to enable users who are not contract authors
to specify information needed for a contract workspace. For example, a sales representative can create a
sales contract request that specifies  the customer and commodity. A contract

ubset of the information used in a contract workspace and include the ability to create a contract workspace
from the information provided. Contract requests are often used to enable users who are not contract authors
to specify information needed for a contract workspace. For example, a sales representative can create a
sales contract request that specifies  the customer and commodity. A contract author can then use the sales
contract request to create a sales contract workspace that contains the information specified  by the sales
representative. The contract request templates provided by SAP Ariba Contracts include a contract workspace
as a subproject that becomes visible when the contract request is approved.
Contract workspaces and requests are both types of SAP Ariba projects. SAP Ariba features for projects are
also supported for contract workspaces and requests except as noted. References in the user interface and
documentation to projects also apply to contract workspaces and requests unless otherwise stated.
Contract Workspace Types
SAP Ariba Contracts supports the following contract workspace types:
•Procurement  contract workspace: a workspace used for contracts with a supplier.
12 PUBLICManaging Projects, T eams, Documents, and T asks
What Is a Project?

•Sales  contract workspace: a workspace used for contracts with a customer or buyer.
•Internal  contract workspace: a workspace intended for contracts within companies or organizations. By
default, inside contract workspaces do not have Supplier  or Buyer  fields.  Instead, they typically include a
custom field  for the employee or other legal party.
All contract workspaces include an Affected  Parties  field  in which you can specify supplier or customer
organizations who are involved in the contract project but not direct participants.
Contract Request Types
SAP Ariba Contracts supports the following contract request types:
•Procurement contract request : a project for requesting a procurement workspace.
•Sales contract request : a project for requesting a sales workspace.
Procurement Project Types
SAP Ariba Buying supports the following project types:
•Procurement Workspaces
A procurement workspace is a project that acts as a single repository for all documents relating to a project,
such as requisitions, contracts, purchase orders, invoices, receipts, and so forth.
•Procurement Workspace Requests
A procurement workspace request is a project used to request a procurement workspace. When a request
is approved, a procurement workspace can be created. Procurement workspace requests usually contain a
subset of the information used in a procurement workspace and include the ability to create a procurement
workspace from the information provided.
Procurement workspaces and procurement workspace requests are both types of SAP Ariba projects.
Project Components
The main components of projects are:
•Overview
Attributes about the project, such as the name, owner, suppliers or customers, and start and end dates.
SAP Ariba Contracts
•Documents
Documents, such as planning documents, savings tracking documents, and forms.
In certain types of SAP Ariba Sourcing projects (full projects), a project can contain multiple sourcing events in
the Documents  area.
In procurement workspaces, this tab can contain requisitions and associated purchase orders, invoices, and
receipts. Y ou can click on the document link to open any of these documents from the Documents  tab. All
documents on the Documents  tab are organized in folders that exist only when there is at least one document
Managing Projects, T eams, Documents, and T asks
What Is a Project? PUBLIC 13

of that type. For example, if there is at least one invoice, there will be a folder called Invoices. However, if there is
no invoice associated with the procurement workspace, there will not be an Invoices folder on the Documents
tab.
SAP Ariba supports documents in multiple formats, including Microsoft Word, ASCII text, and Microsoft Excel.
•T asks
Actions assigned to users, such as reviewing, approving, or negotiating. These tasks enable you to collaborate
with internal or external users to review and revise project information. SAP Ariba tasks include features to
specify workflow  and approval orders and to help you track review versions and comments.
•T eam
The users who are allowed to access different  areas of the project and perform different  actions, such as
modifying or reviewing documents.
•Message Board
A collection of messages for the project. Users can create topics (messages), add replies to messages, and
label messages for filtering.  The system creates a unique email address for each project message board.
Internal and external users who know the address can send messages to the project message board’s email
address for posting to the message board, so that project team members can collaborate with other internal
users, suppliers, and interested outside parties.
•History
A history of the project and its components, such as updated document versions. The history includes the
name of the user who performed each change.
A project can also include links to related documents and knowledge projects.

Contract Project Creation
In SAP Ariba Contracts, you can create projects (contract requests or contract workspaces) using project
templates that can be pre-populated with team members, documents, tasks, and other project items. The available
templates can change depending on the project information (attributes) you specify. Project templates can also
contain conditions that determine the documents, tasks, and phases created according to the project attributes.
If your SAP Ariba solution includes both SAP Ariba Sourcing and SAP Ariba Contracts, you can create a new
contract workspace project directly from a SAP Ariba Sourcing event award. Contract workspaces created from
event awards automatically include commodity, region, supplier, effective  date, and expiration date information
from the event, as well as a Microsoft Excel document containing pricing information. See the Event Management
Guide  for details on creating contract workspaces from the Award  tab in a SAP Ariba Sourcing event.

Creating Contract Projects
Use this procedure to create contract projects.
Prerequisites
•Define  all of the project’s requirements and make sure that the available templates meet those requirements.
Y ou might need to ask your template administrator to create additional templates.
•Select the appropriate project template.
•Review the supplementary documents that you want to include in the project.
•T o associate predecessor project information with the contract, ensure that the following prerequisites are
met:
•Y our SAP Ariba Contracts instance must be integrated with SAP Ariba Sourcing.
•Y our administrator must enable the Enable predecessor event
identification  for contracts created from guided sourcing projects
(IdentifyPredecessorEventforContractsCreatedFromGuidedSourcingProjects ) ICM
parameter. For more information, refer to Enable predecessor event identification  for contracts created
from guided sourcing projects .
Procedure
1. On the dashboard, click Create  and one of the following project types:
•Contract Request (Internal)
•Contract Request (Procurement)
•Contract Request (Sales)
•Contract Workspace (Procurement)
•Contract Workspace (Sales)
If you do not know what type of project to create, see Project T ypes [page 11] .
2. Enter a name for the workspace in the Name  field.
3. (Optional) Enter a description of the workspace.
4. Select a value for the T est Project  field.  In most cases, No is the appropriate value. Specify Y es if you are
creating a project for internal testing or training. For more information, see Contract Request and Contract
Workspace Fields [page 19] .
 Note
Y ou cannot change the test project setting after you have created a project.
Y ou cannot create contract term documents (links to SAP Ariba procurement and invoicing compliance
contracts) in test projects.
5. Select the contract term type from the T erm Type  pull-down menu.
16 PUBLICManaging Projects, T eams, Documents, and T asks
Project Creation

6. Specify a value for the Effective  Date  (the date the contract goes into effect).  Y ou can change this value later, if
needed.
7 .Optional: Select a predecessor project of the contract from the Predecessor Project  pull-down menu.
The predecessor project is a guided sourcing full project. When you select a predecessor project, the option to
select a predecessor event becomes available.
8.Optional: Select a predecessor event of the contract by choosing the [select]  option with the Predecessor
Event  field.
The Select Predecessor Event  page appears where all the subprojects in the selected predecessor project are
listed.
9.Optional: Select a subproject from the list.
If you dont find  the desired subproject in the list or if you dont want to associate a predecessor event, select
(no value) .
 Note
If you select this value, no predecessor event is selected. But the contract workspace is automatically
associated with all the subprojects in the selected predecessor project.
10. Select values for the remaining contract fields.
For more information about these fields,  see Contract Request and Contract Workspace Fields [page 19] .
11. Select a template for the project. The templates that are available depend on the project information you have
entered and the user groups you belong to.
12. Answer any questions shown. The questions are determined by the project template.
13. Click Create .
Results
The contract workspace is created. T o review the information added to the contract in detail, open the contract
workspace in the Full View  mode. For information about changing the contract workspace view, refer to Changing
the Project View [page 58]
 Note
If you have associated predecessor project information with the contract, only a link is established between the
selected guided sourcing projects and the contract workspace. No information is transferred from the guided
sourcing project to the contract workspace.
Next Steps
•Configure  SAP Ariba Contracts to automatically send email notifications  before contract workspaces expire.
See Editing Contract Dates (T erms) and Notification  Schedules [page 66] .
•Add team members to project groups [page 134] , or create additional project groups. Depending on the
template you used to create the project, some team members might already belong to the project.

•Add documents [page 139]  to the project.
•Set up tasks [page 307] . Y ou can submit any tasks inherited from the project template or create and submit
new tasks.
View the Tasks  tab and verify that the phases and tasks associated with your project are correct, and that the
order and due dates of the tasks are accurate. Check to see that team members are responsible for tasks that
match their areas of expertise. Create any additional tasks that your project requires.
Drag and drop phases and tasks on the Tasks  tab in the order in which you intend to perform the tasks. Y ou
cannot modify or delete tasks that were provided by the template. Note that the use of predecessor tasks can
change the order in which you perform tasks.
The Completion column lists the completion dates for phases and tasks. Y ou can set the completion date to a
specific  date, or base it on the start date of the task’s parent phase.
•Publish the project [page 70] . When all of the required tasks are completed and the documents are either
published or in the draft state and ready for publication, you publish the project.
A published, executed contract workspace project remains in effect  until it expires, is closed, is amended to
another contract, or is terminated. See Amending a Published Contract Workspace [page 77] .

Workspaces
Use this procedure to create contract workspaces from procurement workspaces.
Prerequisites

workspace.

team to create a contract workspace from a procurement workspace.
Procedure
1. Open the procurement workspace.

3. Click Actions  and do either of the following:

•T o create a sales contract workspace, click Create Sales Contract .

Workspace (Sales)  page. For information about the fields  on this page, see Creating Contract Projects [page

A link to the contract workplace is added to Documents  tab of the procurement workspace.

T erm Type     X The contract term type. Valid values:
•Fixed
A contract workspace that expires after it reaches its expiration date. After the
expiration date has passed, you can only extend a fixed  contract workspace by
amending it and changing the expiration date.
•Perpetual
A contract workspace that never expires; also known as an evergreen contract. Even
though a perpetual contract never expires, you can set an expiration date and use
that date with notification  attributes to send email notifications  as described in
Editing Contract Dates (T erms) and Notification  Schedules [page 66] . For example,
you could send a reminder to have users review the contract 30 days before the
specified  expiration date.
•Auto Renew
A contract workspace that is automatically renewed (extended) past its expiration
date. The first  renewal occurs on the original expiration date. SAP Ariba updates the
expiration date for each renewal. After the last expiration date has passed, you must
use a renewal amendment type to extend it.

Sourcing Project and Request Creation
Sourcing projects are used to manage sourcing events: Request for Information (RFI) events, Request for Proposal
(RFP) events, auctions, and forward auctions. A sourcing project can be either a quick project (single sourcing
event) or a full project (one or more sourcing events, with full project management features).
Sourcing projects can also be created from sourcing requests. A sourcing request typically includes information
about goods or services that a user or department wants to source. A sourcing project or event can then be created
from the request and the information from the sourcing request is propagated to a sourcing project or event.
Sourcing requests can be created:
•Directly from the SAP Ariba dashboard. All SAP Ariba buyer users can create sourcing requests from the
dashboard.
•Using the optional RFQ and Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature. This feature is an integration
between your ERP system, SAP Business Network, and SAP Ariba Sourcing.
With the RFQ and Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature, a sourcing request is created in SAP
Ariba Sourcing after the following sequence of events:
•an ERP user creates an RFQ
•if the RFQ was created on an Oracle RFQ, the RFQ status is set to Active
•the ERP system sends information about the sourcing request through the SAP Business Network
Events created from sourcing request projects can be awarded to one or multiple suppliers. If your ERP system
is configured  with SAP Business Network and SAP Ariba Sourcing, event award information is sent back to your
ERP system.
After a sourcing event (or project) is created from an ERP sourcing request, any change or delete requests
from the ERP have no effect  on the event (or project) contents or state. SAP Ariba Sourcing sends an email
notification  to all active team members about the request and creates a log entry about the request.
For more information about the RFQ and Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature, see RFQ and
Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing .
•Using the optional Quick Sourcing for Requisitions feature. This feature enables you to create a sourcing
request from a purchase requisition (PR)

mail
notification  to all active team members about the request and creates a log entry about the request.
For more information about the RFQ and Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature, see RFQ and
Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing .
•Using the optional Quick Sourcing for Requisitions feature. This feature enables you to create a sourcing
request from a purchase requisition (PR) created in SAP Ariba Buying or SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing.
By default, sourcing requests created from a PR cannot be canceled or deleted from the SAP Ariba Sourcing
user interface and must be canceled from SAP Ariba Buying or SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing.
Managing Projects, T eams, Documents, and T asks
Project Creation PUBLIC 25

 Limitations
Automatic synchronization is not supported between a sourcing request initiated in SAP Ariba Buying and
Invoicing and the corresponding sourcing project created in SAP Ariba Sourcing.
If you edit the line items in the sourcing project, you must manually update the corresponding purchase
requisition in SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing to maintain consistency between the documents.
For more information about the Quick Sourcing for Requisitions feature, see Quick Sourcing for Requisitions .
If your organization has both SAP Ariba Sourcing and SAP Ariba Contracts, you can create a new contract
workspace project directly from an SAP Ariba Sourcing event award. Contract workspaces created from event
awards automatically include commodity, region, supplier, effective  date, and expiration date information from the
event, as well as a Microsoft Excel document containing pricing information. See the Event Management Guide  for
details on creating contract workspaces from the Award  tab in a SAP Ariba Sourcing event.
For an overview of project management features, see Introduction to Projects [page 11] ; for information specifically
about creating and managing SAP Ariba Sourcing events, see the Event Management Guide .

Creating Sourcing Requests from the Dashboard
All users can use this procedure to create a sourcing request from the dashboard, regardless of their group
membership. A sourcing request can be used to store event information that is propagated to a sourcing project or
event created from the request.
Prerequisites
Y ou must have a project template for creating a sourcing request from the dashboard. When creating a sourcing
request from the dashboard, you cannot use the template provided for creating sourcing requests with an
ERP integration. The template used to create a sourcing request from the dashboard must meet the following
requirements:
•The value of Origin  field  must be Ariba .
•The template must not have the visibility condition (Origin)is equal to(External))  (the IsExternalOrigin
condition) . This condition is present in the template for sourcing requests created using the RFQ and Award
Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature.
Procedure
1. On the dashboard, click 
 Create
 Sourcing Request
 .
The Create Sourcing Request  page opens.
2. Enter the details of your requirement.
3. Select the sourcing request template.
4. Click Create.
The Sourcing Request page opens in Full View  .
5. (Optional) By default, the sourcing request contains a Sourcing Request Items  document that can store
event information (rules, team, suppliers, and content. Information in the Sourcing Request Items  document
is propagated to the event or project created from this request. If the sourcing request is created from the
dashboard, you can edit the Sourcing Request Items  document.
 Note
If the sourcing request was created from the RFQ and Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature,
you cannot edit the Sourcing Request Items  document.
a. Under the Documents  tab, click the Sourcing Request Items  document. Select 
 Action 
 View
Details
 .

The Sourcing Request Items  document opens.

c. Enter a description and specify values for Base Language , Currency , and Commodity  as appropriate.

Y ou can enter information for the Sourcing Request Items  as you would for an event, beginning with the
event rules.
6. View and submit the tasks under the Tasks  tab. Click on a task name and select View Details . Complete tasks
assigned to you.
By default, sourcing requests have an Approval for Sourcing Request  approval task. This task is created
for the entire request; when it is approved, a placeholder for a sourcing project becomes visible under the
Documents  tab and a sourcing event can be created. By default, the Approval for Sourcing Request  task has
an empty approval flow  and will be automatically approved when it is submitted. If you do not want this task to
be automatically approved, edit the task and add approvers to the flow  before submitting it.
Next Steps
In most cases, a sourcing subproject will be visible in the Documents  tab after the approval task for the request
is approved. A user with the ability to create sourcing projects can click on this subproject and create a sourcing
project (full sourcing project or quick project).

If you have the RFQ and Award Integration with SAP Ariba Sourcing feature configured,  SAP Ariba creates sourcing
requests based on data received from the ERP system. Use this procedure to process those requests.
Context
By default, members of the Sourcing Manager  group receive an email notification  when a sourcing event is
created from an integration event. The email notification  includes the name of the sourcing request. These sourcing
requests include, by default, required tasks that must be completed before a sourcing project or event can be
created from the request.
 Tip
If you are an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition user, you can configure  the  Sourcing with SAP Ariba
Sourcing (4BL)  integration or the Central Procurement with SAP Ariba Sourcing (4QN)  to enable Ariba Sourcing
integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
•For information about the 4BL workflow,  see Sourcing with SAP Ariba Sourcing (4BL) .
•For information about the 4QN workflow,  see Central Procurement with SAP Ariba Sourcing (4QN) .
 Note
The documentation links to the 4BL and 4QN workflow  topics point to the English version of the SAP S/
4HANA Cloud Public Edition documentation. If you prefer a different  language, use the language selection
option at the top of the page to switch to the language of your choice.
Procedure
1. Locate the sourcing request. Y ou can use the name of the request in the email notification  as a search field.
2. Add supporting documents if needed. Open the Documents  tab and select the appropriate option in the
Actions  menu.
3. View and submit the tasks under the Tasks  tab. Click on a task name and select View Details . Complete tasks
assigned to you.
By default, sourcing requests have an Approval for Sourcing Request  approval task. This task is created
for the entire request; when it is approved, a placeholder for a sourcing project becomes visible under the
Documents  tab and a sourcing event can be created. By default, the Approval for Sourcing Request  task has
an empty approval flow  and will be automatically approved when it is submitted. If you do not want this task to
be automatically approved, edit the task and add approvers to the flow  before submitting it.

participants.
Predecessor Project Choosing a predecessor project from the Predecessor Project  pull-down menu creates
this project as a follow-on project to an existing project. Fields in the current project that
are also present in the predecessor project will be pre-populated with values from the
predecessor project.
CommodityThe commodities for the project or event. Y our company might refer to commodities as
categories, UNSPSC codes, or by another term. An example of a commodity is office
supplies. The values for this field  can be used to pre-populate projects with team mem-
bers, content, and approvers specific  to the commodity.
Owner The project owner. The default is the user creating the project.

Creation of Procurement Workspaces and
Procurement Workspace Requests
This topic is applicable only for SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing and SAP Ariba Buying users.
Y ou create procurement workspace requests and procurement workspaces using templates that represent your
company’s standard business processes and can be pre-populated with team members, documents, tasks, and
other project items. The available templates can change depending on the project information (attributes) you
specify. Project templates can also contain conditions that determine the documents, tasks, and phases created
according to the project attributes. For information about creating project templates, see Project T emplate Guide .
 Note
Procurement workspace requests and procurement workspaces are created as projects on your SAP Ariba
system and may also be referred to as projects in this guide and in other SAP Ariba documentation.
Procurement workspaces can be created from any of the following starting points:
•Procurement workspace requests

•The SAP Ariba dashboard
•The Summary  page of a requisition
•An existing procurement workspace
Members of the Procurement Project Creator  group can create procurement workspaces from any of these
points. If you are not a member of the Procurement Project Creator  group, but you are a member of the
Procurement Project Requestor group, you must first  create a procurement workspace request to propose a
project. If the request is approved, you can create a procurement workspace from the request.
Before you create a project, SAP Ariba recommends that you:
•Define  all of the project’s requirements and make sure that the available templates meet those requirements.
Y ou might need to ask your template administrator to create additional templates.
•Select the appropriate project template.
•Consider the supplemental documents you want to include in the project.
